key_insight: GrapNet treats the neural network itself as an editable graph program, so relations, frozen parts, routing, and execution backends can be changed structurally rather than by ad hoc weight surgery.
engineering_relevance: This makes neural models more controllable and auditable for solid mechanics and other engineering workflows that need modular encoders, partial freezing, and topology-aware adaptation.
method: Child-owned graph nodes store next-layer references plus a trainable allocation vector aligned to those references.
Structural operations can delete, freeze, grow, regroup, or route active relations without changing the node core.
The same substrate can interface with dense layers, CNNs, ResNets, attention blocks, and transformers through vector-valued parent inputs.
evidence_signal: In matched ten-seed studies, GrapNet improved Split Fashion-MNIST seen-class accuracy by 12.08 points over a larger dense MLP+ER, and improved Split CIFAR-10 online head accuracy by 3.81 points with statistically significant p-values.
watch_item: Because behavior depends on explicit structural edits and execution policies, correctness and reproducibility may be sensitive to implementation details when the graph topology changes.
paper_title: GrapNet: A Programmable Dynamic-Architecture Neural Graph Substrate
